Grapevine_Black_Rot.definition: A highly destructive fungal disease caused by Guignardia bidwellii that can devastate an entire vineyard if left unchecked. It thrives in warm, humid environments and attacks all green parts of the vine.
Grapevine_Black_Rot.symptoms: Initial symptoms appear as small, circular tan spots on leaves that develop a dark brown border. As it progresses, fruit becomes infected, shriveling into hard, black 'mummies' that look like raisins covered in tiny black bumps.
Grapevine_Black_Rot.causes: Overwintering fungal spores in old fruit mummies or infected canes are released by spring rains. High humidity and leaf wetness for 6-7 hours are required for infection.
Grapevine_Black_Rot.cure_steps: 1. Manually prune and destroy all 'mummy' fruit and infected canes. 2. Apply systemic fungicides immediately. 3. Ensure high trellis systems to improve airflow. 4. Clear all leaf litter from the vineyard floor.
Grapevine_Black_Rot.pesticide_schedule: Apply Mancozeb or Myclobutanil-based fungicides starting at 1-inch shoot growth. Repeat every 10–14 days until berries reach 5% sugar content (veraison).
Grapevine_Black_Rot.fertilizer_schedule: Maintain moderate Nitrogen levels; excessive Nitrogen can cause lush canopy growth which traps humidity. Use a balanced 10-10-10 NPK mix in early spring.
Grapevine_Black_Rot.water_schedule: Use drip irrigation only. Avoid overhead sprinklers which keep leaves wet and accelerate fungal germination.
Grapevine_Black_Rot.preventive_measures: Strict dormant season pruning and sanitation. Select resistant varieties like 'Norton' or 'Chancellor' for new plantings.
Grapevine_Black_Rot.expected_recovery: 75% (Focus on saving the current vine and protecting the next season's fruit set).

Grapevine_ESCA.definition: Also known as 'Black Measles,' Esca is a complex trunk disease caused by a 'cocktail' of fungi (Phaeomoniella and Phaeoacremonium). It slowly rots the woody interior of the vine.
Grapevine_ESCA.symptoms: Leaves show 'tiger-stripe' chlorosis (yellowing between veins that turns reddish-brown). Berries develop dark, 'measle-like' spots. In acute cases (Apoplexy), the entire vine may wilt and die within days.
Grapevine_ESCA.causes: Fungal pathogens enter through large pruning wounds during wet winters. The fungi colonize the water-conducting tissue, slowly starving the plant.
Grapevine_ESCA.cure_sets: There is no chemical cure for the trunk infection. 1. Cut back the trunk to healthy white wood. 2. Re-train a new sucker from the base. 3. Seal all large pruning cuts with wound paste.
Grapevine_ESCA.pesticide_schedule: Pesticides are ineffective against the internal rot. Use wound sealants (like Tesium or Garrand) immediately after pruning to prevent new infections.
Grapevine_ESCA.fertilizer_schedule: Apply Bio-stimulants and foliar micronutrients (Zinc/Boron) to reduce vine stress and help the plant cope with reduced water transport.
Grapevine_ESCA.water_schedule: Increase irrigation frequency but decrease volume. The vine’s 'plumbing' is damaged, so it needs a steady, easy supply of water.
Grapevine_ESCA.preventive_measures: Avoid pruning during rainy weather. Practice 'double pruning' and always disinfect pruning shears with a 10% bleach solution between vines.
Grapevine_ESCA.expected_recovery: 40-60% (Depends on the age of the vine and extent of wood necrosis).
Grapevine_ESCA.treatment_cost: 55 USD (includes labor for surgical wood removal)

Grapevine_Healthy.definition: The specimen shows optimal physiological health with no signs of fungal, bacterial, or viral pathogens.
Grapevine_Healthy.symptoms: Uniform deep green leaves, vigorous shoot extension, and firm fruit with intact bloom. No spotting, curling, or discoloration present.
Grapevine_Healthy.causes: N/A - Optimal growing conditions and successful management.
Grapevine_Healthy.cure_steps: Maintain current management practices. Focus on scouting for early signs of pests like leafhoppers or mites.
Grapevine_Healthy.pesticide_schedule: No curative fungicides needed. Maintain a standard preventive sulfur spray if humidity rises above 80%.
Grapevine_Healthy.fertilizer_schedule: Apply a maintenance dose of composted manure or balanced NPK based on your latest soil test results.
Grapevine_Healthy.water_schedule: Continue standard deep-watering schedule (approx. 1 inch per week depending on soil type).
Grapevine_Healthy.preventive_measures: Keep records of growth stages. Ensure canopy management (leaf pulling) to maintain light penetration and airflow.

Grapevine_Leaf_Blight.definition: Caused by Pseudocercospora vitis, this disease primarily affects the foliage, reducing the plant's ability to photosynthesize and ripen fruit.
Grapevine_Leaf_Blight.symptoms: Irregular, reddish-brown spots appear on the leaf surface. As the disease progresses, the leaf margins may curl and the entire leaf may drop prematurely (defoliation).
Grapevine_Leaf_Blight.causes: Persistent humidity and temperatures between 20°C and 28°C. Spores spread via wind and splashing rain.
Grapevine_Leaf_Blight.cure_steps: 1. Apply Copper-based fungicides. 2. Remove heavily blighted leaves to reduce spore load. 3. Thin the canopy to increase sunlight exposure.
Grapevine_Leaf_Blight.pesticide_schedule: Apply Copper Oxychloride or Boscalid-based sprays every 7–10 days during rainy periods.
Grapevine_Leaf_Blight.fertilizer_schedule: Avoid high Nitrogen. Use Potassium-heavy fertilizers to thicken leaf cell walls, making them harder for fungi to penetrate.
Grapevine_Leaf_Blight.water_schedule: Irrigate in the early morning so leaves have time to dry during the day. Avoid evening watering.
Grapevine_Leaf_Blight.preventive_measures: Space vines properly for air circulation. Clear weeds around the base of the vine which can act as secondary hosts.

Corn_healthy.definition: Corn specimen is in peak condition. High chlorophyll content and strong stalk integrity observed.
Corn_healthy.symptoms: Vibrant green leaves, strong upright stalk, and well-developed root system. No evidence of blight, rust, or borer damage.
Corn_healthy.cure_steps: Maintain nitrogen monitoring, especially during the silking stage. Continue scouting for insect pests.
Corn_healthy.pesticide_schedule: N/A - Monitor for corn borers or aphids weekly.
Corn_healthy.fertilizer_schedule: Side-dress with Nitrogen (Urea) when the corn is at the V6 growth stage (knee-high).
Corn_healthy.water_schedule: Critical watering periods: Silking and Tasseling. Ensure at least 1.5 inches of water per week during these stages.

Corn_Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray_leaf_spot.definition: One of the most significant yield-limiting diseases of corn globally. It thrives in high humidity and minimum tillage systems.
Corn_Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray_leaf_spot.symptoms: Long, narrow, rectangular lesions that run parallel to leaf veins. These spots appear gray to tan and can merge to kill the entire leaf blade.
Corn_Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray_leaf_spot.causes: The fungus overwinters in corn residue on the soil surface. Spores are splashed by rain onto lower leaves.
Corn_Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray_leaf_spot.cure_steps: 1. Apply foliar fungicides early (V12 to R1 stage). 2. Transition to conventional tillage to bury infected residue. 3. Harvest early if stalk integrity is failing.
Corn_Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray_leaf_spot.pesticide_schedule: Apply Strobilurin or Triazole fungicides (like Headline or Quilt Xcel) at the first sign of lesions on the ear leaf.
Corn_Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray_leaf_spot.fertilizer_schedule: Ensure adequate Potassium levels. Potassium helps the corn stalk stay strong even when the leaves are under stress.
Corn_Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray_leaf_spot.water_schedule: Standard irrigation; however, avoid irrigation during late evening to keep leaf wetness duration low.
Corn_Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray_leaf_spot.preventive_measures: Crop rotation for at least 2 years (rotate with Soybeans or Wheat). Use hybrids with high GL_Spot resistance scores.
Corn_Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray_leaf_spot.expected_recovery: 65-70% (Yield loss is mitigated if treated before silking).

Corn_Common_rust.definition: Caused by the fungus Puccinia sorghi. While often considered a minor disease, it can become severe in cool, moist summers.
Corn_Common_rust.symptoms: Small, cinnamon-brown, circular to elongated pustules (blisters) on both the upper and lower leaf surfaces. These pustules contain powdery spores that rub off on skin.
Corn_Common_rust.causes: Spores do not overwinter in cold climates; they are blown in from warmer southern regions every year via wind currents.
Corn_Common_rust.cure_steps: 1. Immediate application of Triazole fungicides. 2. Monitor high-value seed corn crops especially closely. 3. Most field corn can tolerate low levels, but treatment is needed if it reaches the ear leaf.
Corn_Common_rust.pesticide_schedule: Apply fungicides only if pustules appear before the tasseling stage. Repeat every 7-14 days if cool, wet weather persists.
Corn_Common_rust.fertilizer_schedule: Balanced NPK. Avoid late-season Nitrogen spikes which can favor fungal growth.
Corn_Common_rust.water_schedule: Regular irrigation. Ensure the field has good drainage to reduce local humidity within the crop canopy.
Corn_Common_rust.preventive_measures: The most effective control is planting 'Rp-resistant' hybrids. Destroy volunteer corn plants in early spring.
Corn_Common_rust.expected_recovery: 80% (If treated early; rust spreads very fast).

Corn_Northern_Leaf_Blight.definition: A major fungal disease (Exserohilum turcicum) that causes large, cigar-shaped lesions on leaves, leading to massive loss in photosynthetic area.
Corn_Northern_Leaf_Blight.symptoms: Large (1-6 inch) cigar-shaped, grayish-green to tan lesions. These usually start on lower leaves and move upwards. In wet weather, lesions may look 'dirty' due to spore production.
Corn_Northern_Leaf_Blight.causes: Favorable conditions include moderate temperatures (18-27°C) and prolonged heavy dews.
Corn_Northern_Leaf_Blight.cure_steps: 1. Use fungicides at silking. 2. Prune heavily infected lower leaves in small-scale farming. 3. Manage residue via tillage to reduce the local spore source.
Corn_Northern_Leaf_Blight.pesticide_schedule: Apply fungicides like Delaro or Trivapro at the V12-VT stage. A second application may be required if rain continues.
Corn_Northern_Leaf_Blight.fertilizer_schedule: Optimize Nitrogen levels; nitrogen-stressed corn is significantly more susceptible to blight.
Corn_Northern_Leaf_Blight.water_schedule: Avoid any form of overhead irrigation if possible. Use furrow or drip irrigation to keep moisture at the soil level.
Corn_Northern_Leaf_Blight.preventive_measures: 2-year crop rotation. Choose hybrids with Ht-genes for specific resistance to Northern Blight races.
Corn_Northern_Leaf_Blight.expected_recovery: 50-60% (Depends heavily on how early the infection reaches the ear-leaf).

Tomato_Bacterial_spot.definition: A severe bacterial infection caused by Xanthomonas species. It is highly contagious and spreads rapidly in warm, rainy weather, affecting all above-ground parts of the plant.
Tomato_Bacterial_spot.symptoms: Small, water-soaked, greasy-looking spots on leaves that eventually turn black with a yellow halo. Infected fruit develops raised, scab-like spots that make it unmarketable.
Tomato_Bacterial_spot.causes: Bacteria overwinter on plant debris and seeds. It is spread via splashing rain, overhead irrigation, and contaminated garden tools.
Tomato_Bacterial_spot.cure_steps: 1. Prune lower branches to improve airflow and reduce soil splash. 2. Apply copper-based bactericides immediately. 3. Remove and burn heavily infected plants to prevent field-wide spread.
Tomato_Bacterial_spot.pesticide_schedule: Apply Fixed Copper or Streptomycin sprays every 7-10 days. If conditions are very wet, reduce the interval to 5 days.
Tomato_Bacterial_spot.fertilizer_schedule: Avoid high Nitrogen side-dressing during an outbreak, as lush new growth is highly susceptible. Use a balanced 10-10-10 fertilizer.
Tomato_Bacterial_spot.water_schedule: Strictly use drip or furrow irrigation. Watering must be done at the base; never wet the foliage during an outbreak.
Tomato_Bacterial_spot.preventive_measures: Purchase only certified disease-free seeds. Practice a 3-year rotation away from peppers and tomatoes. Sanitize tools with 70% ethanol.
Tomato_Bacterial_spot.expected_recovery: 60% (Fruit quality is usually affected even if the plant survives).

Tomato_Early_blight.definition: A common fungal disease caused by Alternaria solani. It typically starts on the older, lower leaves and moves upward, causing significant defoliation.
Tomato_Early_blight.symptoms: Characteristic 'target' spots—brown circular lesions with distinct concentric rings. Leaves eventually turn yellow and drop, exposing fruit to sunscald.
Tomato_Early_blight.causes: The fungus survives in soil and crop residue. It thrives in high humidity and temperatures between 24-29°C.
Tomato_Early_blight.cure_steps: 1. Remove infected lower leaves (bottom 12 inches of the plant). 2. Mulch the soil surface to prevent spores from splashing up. 3. Apply chlorothalonil or mancozeb fungicides.
Tomato_Early_blight.pesticide_schedule: Preventive spray of Chlorothalonil every 7 days. Switch to Copper-based sprays if the disease persists during harvest.
Tomato_Early_blight.fertilizer_schedule: Maintain high soil fertility; vigorous plants tolerate early blight better. Ensure adequate Potassium to strengthen cell walls.
Tomato_Early_blight.water_schedule: Morning irrigation only to allow foliage to dry quickly. Ensure proper plant spacing for air circulation.
Tomato_Early_blight.preventive_measures: Stake and prune plants to keep foliage off the ground. Use a 2-year crop rotation and remove all nightshade weeds.
Tomato_Early_blight.expected_recovery: 55-70% (Success depends on keeping the top 1/3 of the canopy clean).

Tomato_Late_blight.definition: The most destructive tomato disease (Phytophthora infestans), the same pathogen responsible for the Irish Potato Famine. It can kill a healthy field in days.
Tomato_Late_blight.symptoms: Large, irregular greenish-black 'water-soaked' patches on leaves. In humid weather, a white fuzzy mold appears on the underside of leaves. Fruit develops firm, chocolate-brown lesions.
Tomato_Late_blight.causes: Airborne spores travel miles on wind and rain. It requires cool, moist nights and warm days to explode into an epidemic.
Tomato_Late_blight.cure_steps: 1. Act immediately; late blight is a 'community disease'. 2. Apply systemic fungicides like Ridomil Gold. 3. If more than 50% of the plant is affected, pull and bag the entire plant immediately.
Tomato_Late_blight.pesticide_schedule: Professional-grade fungicides (Mancozeb or Copper) every 5 days during high-risk weather (foggy/rainy).
Tomato_Late_blight.fertilizer_schedule: Foliar Phosphorus can help boost the plant's immune response, but chemical control is the only effective measure.
Tomato_Late_blight.water_schedule: Do not irrigate during periods of high humidity. Minimize all moisture on the plant canopy.
Tomato_Late_blight.preventive_measures: Plant resistant varieties (e.g., 'Mountain Magic' or 'Defiant'). Avoid planting tomatoes near potatoes.

Tomato_Leaf_Mold.definition: Caused by the fungus Fulvia fulva, this is primarily a disease of high-humidity environments, especially in greenhouses or high-tunnel systems.
Tomato_Leaf_Mold.symptoms: Pale green or yellow spots on the upper leaf surface, with olive-green 'velvety' fungal growth on the corresponding underside.
Tomato_Leaf_Mold.causes: Relative humidity above 85% is required. Spores are spread by wind, workers' clothing, and tools.
Tomato_Leaf_Mold.cure_steps: 1. Increase ventilation immediately by opening greenhouse sides. 2. Prune excess foliage to lower the humidity inside the plant canopy. 3. Apply fungicides containing Difenoconazole.
Tomato_Leaf_Mold.pesticide_schedule: Fungicide application every 7 days until humidity levels can be consistently dropped below 70%.
Tomato_Leaf_Mold.fertilizer_schedule: Balanced NPK. Avoid over-fertilization with Nitrogen, which creates the dense canopy that traps moisture.
Tomato_Leaf_Mold.water_schedule: Use drip tapes under plastic mulch to keep the environment as dry as possible.
Tomato_Leaf_Mold.preventive_measures: Select hybrids with the 'Cf' resistance genes. Maintain a 3-foot spacing between plants.
Tomato_Leaf_Mold.expected_recovery: 65% (Can be managed effectively by controlling air movement).

Tomato_Septoria_leaf_spot.definition: A fungal disease (Septoria lycopersici) that is less lethal than Late Blight but causes massive leaf loss, resulting in poor fruit taste and sunscald.
Tomato_Septoria_leaf_spot.symptoms: Numerous tiny, circular spots (3mm) with dark borders and gray/white centers. Tiny black specks (fruiting bodies) can be seen inside the gray centers.
Tomato_Septoria_leaf_spot.causes: Spores splash from the soil or infected debris onto lower leaves. It is most active during long periods of rainfall.
Tomato_Septoria_leaf_spot.cure_steps: 1. Remove the first infected leaves at the bottom of the plant. 2. Mulch heavily with straw or plastic to create a barrier with the soil. 3. Apply protectant fungicides.
Tomato_Septoria_leaf_spot.pesticide_schedule: Apply Chlorothalonil or Potassium Bicarbonate every 7–10 days.
Tomato_Septoria_leaf_spot.fertilizer_schedule: Apply a side-dressing of Calcium Nitrate to help the plant recover lost foliage quickly.
Tomato_Septoria_leaf_spot.water_schedule: Water early in the morning so the sun dries the leaves quickly. Avoid evening watering.
Tomato_Septoria_leaf_spot.preventive_measures: Deep-plow crop residue at the end of the season. Rotate out of Solanaceous crops for 2 years.
Tomato_Septoria_leaf_spot.expected_recovery: 60% (Plants will continue to produce fruit if the top growth is protected).

Tomato_Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider_m.definition: Microscopic pests (Tetranychus urticae) that suck the sap out of plant cells. They thrive in hot, dry, and dusty conditions.
Tomato_Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider_m.symptoms: Leaves show fine yellow stippling (dots). Under heavy infestation, fine silk webbing covers the growing tips, and leaves turn bronze and dry up.
Tomato_Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider_m.causes: Dry weather and the use of broad-spectrum insecticides that kill the mites' natural predators (like ladybugs).
Tomato_Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider_m.cure_steps: 1. Spray the undersides of leaves with a strong stream of water to knock mites off. 2. Apply Neem oil or Insecticidal soap. 3. Introduce predatory mites (Phytoseiulus persimilis).
Tomato_Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider_m.pesticide_schedule: Apply Abamectin or Neem oil every 5 days for 3 cycles (to kill new hatchlings).
Tomato_Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider_m.fertilizer_schedule: Maintain adequate Nitrogen; drought-stressed and nutrient-deficient plants are more attractive to mites.
Tomato_Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider_m.water_schedule: Mist the plants during midday heat to increase local humidity, which spider mites hate.
Tomato_Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider_m.preventive_measures: Keep the area around the garden weed-free and avoid excessive dust. Monitor with a magnifying glass weekly.
Tomato_Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider_m.expected_recovery: 75% (If webbing hasn't completely covered the plant).

Tomato_Target_Spot.definition: A fungal disease (Corynespora cassiicola) that looks similar to Early Blight but can infect the fruit much more aggressively.
Tomato_Target_Spot.symptoms: Brown-black spots with faint concentric rings. Unlike Early Blight, these spots are often surrounded by a large yellow chlorotic zone.
Tomato_Target_Spot.causes: High humidity and moderate temperatures. It is often introduced via infected transplants.
Tomato_Target_Spot.cure_steps: 1. Remove all infected leaves and fruit. 2. Improve row spacing for better light penetration. 3. Apply fungicides containing Azoxystrobin.
Tomato_Target_Spot.pesticide_schedule: Fungicide sprays every 7 days. Rotate between different chemical classes to prevent resistance.
Tomato_Target_Spot.fertilizer_schedule: Ensure adequate Micronutrients (Boron and Manganese) to support plant defense enzymes.
Tomato_Target_Spot.water_schedule: Drip irrigation only. Keep the plant base clear of weeds to allow the soil surface to dry.
Tomato_Target_Spot.preventive_measures: Inspect all nursery-bought seedlings for spots before planting. Sanitize stakes and cages annually.
Tomato_Target_Spot.expected_recovery: 60% (The fungus can stay in the soil for a long time).

Tomato_healthy.definition: The tomato plant is in an optimal physiological state with a strong vascular system and high photosynthetic efficiency.
Tomato_healthy.symptoms: Turgid, dark green foliage; sturdy, hairy stems; and vibrant yellow flowers. No lesions or pests detected.
Tomato_healthy.cure_steps: Continue current pruning and staking regime. Monitor for the first appearance of hornworms.
Tomato_healthy.pesticide_schedule: N/A - Optional weekly application of organic Neem oil as a general repellent.
Tomato_healthy.fertilizer_schedule: Apply a balanced organic fertilizer every 3 weeks. Ensure consistent Calcium levels to prevent Blossom End Rot.
Tomato_healthy.water_schedule: Consistent moisture (1-2 inches per week). Mulch heavily to prevent moisture fluctuations.
Tomato_healthy.preventive_measures: Maintain 'Clean Garden' policy. Remove any yellowing lower leaves that touch the soil.

Potato_Early_blight.definition: A common fungal disease (Alternaria solani) that primarily affects foliage but can lead to secondary tuber rot if the infection is severe.
Potato_Early_blight.symptoms: Dark, sunken circular spots with 'bullseye' concentric rings on older leaves. Heavily infected leaves turn yellow and die, but stay attached to the stem.
Potato_Early_blight.causes: The fungus overwinters in soil or infected tubers. It spreads via wind and splashing water during alternating wet and dry periods.
Potato_Early_blight.cure_steps: 1. Apply protective fungicides. 2. Harvest tubers only when the vines are completely dead to prevent spore transfer to tubers. 3. Avoid wounding tubers during harvest.
Potato_Early_blight.pesticide_schedule: Apply Mancozeb or Chlorothalonil every 7-10 days, starting when the plants are 6 inches tall.
Potato_Early_blight.fertilizer_schedule: Ensure adequate Nitrogen. Early blight is often called a 'disease of senescence' because it attacks older, nutrient-depleted tissue.
Potato_Early_blight.water_schedule: Avoid overhead sprinklers. Use furrow irrigation to keep the canopy dry.
Potato_Early_blight.preventive_measures: Use certified disease-free seed potatoes. Practice a 3-year rotation (avoiding peppers, eggplants, or tomatoes).
Potato_Early_blight.expected_recovery: 65% (Tuber yield is reduced if defoliation occurs before bulking).

Potato_Late_blight.definition: A catastrophic disease (Phytophthora infestans) that can rot tubers in the ground and destroy the entire canopy in under a week.
Potato_Late_blight.symptoms: Dark, water-soaked lesions on leaves that expand rapidly. A white, velvety fungal growth appears on the leaf underside in humid weather. Tubers show a reddish-brown dry rot.
Potato_Late_blight.causes: High humidity (>90%) and temperatures between 15-21°C. It travels easily through wind-blown rain.
Potato_Late_blight.cure_steps: 1. Destroy all 'cull' potato piles nearby. 2. Apply systemic fungicides like Metalaxyl immediately. 3. If infection is widespread, kill the vines (desiccation) to save the tubers.
Potato_Late_blight.pesticide_schedule: Intensive spray program (every 5 days) using fungicides like Curzate or Revus Top.
Potato_Late_blight.fertilizer_schedule: Balanced NPK. Avoid excessive Nitrogen late in the season, which keeps the canopy green and susceptible longer.
Potato_Late_blight.water_schedule: Eliminate all irrigation during high-risk weather forecasts. Monitor the 'Late Blight Forecast' in your region.
Potato_Late_blight.preventive_measures: Plant resistant cultivars like 'Defender'. Destroy volunteer potato plants in early spring.

Potato_healthy.definition: Potato plant shows high vigor, optimal tuber initiation, and no signs of vascular wilt or foliar pathogens.
Potato_healthy.symptoms: Lush green canopy, upright growth, and healthy white root development. No signs of beetle damage or leaf curling.
Potato_healthy.cure_steps: Maintain 'hilling' (adding soil to the base) to protect tubers from light and pests. Watch for Potato Beetles.
Potato_healthy.pesticide_schedule: N/A - Monitor for aphids which can transmit viruses.
Potato_healthy.fertilizer_schedule: Apply high-potassium fertilizer during the tuber bulking stage to maximize starch content.
Potato_healthy.water_schedule: Maintain even soil moisture. Fluctuating water levels can cause 'hollow heart' or 'growth cracks' in tubers.
Potato_healthy.preventive_measures: Continue hilling and scouting. Ensure tools are clean before moving between fields.
